Are you in again? You're here more than I am! You'll doubtless be happy to know we've had a unit failure at Tonbridge so we need you to take this 375 down as a replacement. It's a bit breezy, but keep an eye out. I'll bet the Medway will be looking not far off it's best today.
As much of a pain as that was, I'll bet the Medway looked pretty stunning...A Winter Wonderland eh? Go and grab a warm drink whilst you wait for your next service, and well done again.

Explore the rural Kent countryside aboard the Southeastern Class 375/3, which have run on the line since 2012, with Southeastern themselves having operated since 2006. The Medway Valley Line also sees rush hour Highspeed services and is a key link for freight and engineers trains.

Hi driver. Bad news I'm afraid, the points ahead are frozen. The only option is to deboard the passengers here, then you'll have to shunt to Platform2. We'll get them over the bridge whilst you're doing that, then you can continue on your way. It's just one of those days isn't it?! Let's get them off the train so you can crack on.

Hi there. I'm sure you're already aware but 2T42 is running very late indeed. When the service pulls in we need a quick changeover so you can get on your way. Important to note you're now express to Strood, stopping only at Maidstone West. Safety first obviously, but any time you can regain will help us later in the day. It should be in soon, good luck.

It’s been an unsteady start this cold winter morning, and the 375s can’t get moving. As one of the first trains to make it out of Tonbridge Jubilee Sidings, you’re taking this entire 6-car train to Strood in the best hope of keeping traction.

Morning driver, don't you just love the rain? No? Me neither. Anyway, make your way over to Jubilee Sidings to get sorted out and set up for today.

Morning driver, how's it going? You're aware of your job today I gather, It's an end-to-end return track inspection on the Medway Valley. The weather could have been better but it is what it is, you can still get the job done. The engineers haven't had chance yet so remember, before you go, you need to attach the camera. Have a good one and we'll see you back here later on. Cheerio.

Morning driver. What a night we've had! This snow isn't showing any signs of letting up and the forecast is for it to get worse throughout the day. Anyway, on to brighter things, lucky you!! You've got the first train out of Jubilee Sidings as the points have been frozen all night. We're having to double up, so you're in a 6 car 375/3 all the way to Strood. Be mindful of your stopping points and keep an eye out, you're the first northbound service up the Medway Valley today.

The Medway Valley Line was completed in two stages. The first opened in 1847 and connected Maidstone to the South Eastern Railway (SER) at Paddock Wood, followed by the extension to Strood in 1856. As its name suggests, the tracks closely follow the path of the River Medway for almost its entire length.

The Medway Valley Line was completed in two stages. The first opened in 1847 and connected Maidstone to the South Eastern Railway (SER) at Paddock Wood, followed by the extension to Strood in 1856. As its name suggests, the tracks closely follow the path of the River Medway for almost its entire length.

The line features several unique quirks. The semaphore signalling was replaced in the 2000s but the locally staffed signalboxes remain to this day. There are grade-listed buildings and structures across its entire length, including an abundance of old Kentish Oast Houses - a glimpse into the line's hop-brewing past. The line's scenic nature means there are many footcrossings too, resulting in a high concentration of whistle boards.

Welcome back driver, how was it? Not too slippery I hope? Excellent job anyway, that should give us all the data we need, we'll get that off to the engineers as soon as we can. A good job well done I'd say. I've got to get on now, I'll catch you later.
Welcome to the Medway Valley, let's get started.
Well done completeling the chapter, you now have some experience under your belt. The Medway Valley is very pretty at night isn't it?

Well, you don't see that very often! Have you experienced many emergency stops before? I've only ever seen the one myself. Anyway, great job responding to it and keeping everyone safe. Looks like you'll be here for a while, getting those sheep rounded up and accounted for will be no small job. No doubt you'll have some paperwork to do so I'll leave you to it...
